contents: Original articleCEDClinical and Experimental DermatologyAssociation between plasma interleukin-17 levels and risk ofpsoriasis: a meta-analysisX. Y. Zhou, J. Bao, B. Huang and Y. JinSchool of Pharmacy, Anhui Medical University, Hefei, Anhui, China

SummaryBackground. Emerging evidence indicates that plasma interleukin (IL)-17 levelsmay be associated with increased risk of psoriasis, but the individual publishedresults are inconclusive.Aim. To evaluate IL-17 levels in patients with psoriasis using a meta-analysis ofstudies comparing IL-17 levels in controls and in patients with psoriasis.Methods. All relevant studies were identified by searching PubMed, Web of Scienceand MEDLINE databases before 1 November 2015. Pooled risk estimates were calcu-lated by random-effects models. Crude OR and standardized mean difference (SMD)with corresponding 95% CI were also calculated.Results. In total, eight cross-sectional study studies were included in the final an-alysis. The mean plasma levels of IL-17 were higher in patients with psoriasis thanin healthy controls (SMD = 0.47, 95% CI = 0.07-0.86, P < 0.001).Conclusions. Based on this literature review, there appears to be a positive associa-tion between IL-17 plasma levels and psoriasis.IntroductionPsoriasis is a cutaneous inflammatory disease thataffects 2-5% of the population worldwide.1 The typicalpresentation of psoriasis includes erythematous andsilvery-white scaly skin plaques, found mainly on exten-sor areas, and there can also be dystrophic changes inthe nails. Based on the the latest literature, psoriasis isnow considered a chronic, systemic inflammatory disor-der. It also appears to be a variably dominant, geneti-cally transmitted disease of multifactorial aetiology.2Interleukin (IL)-17 is a cytokine produced by mastcells, innate lymphoid cells (ILCs), unconventionalT cells and typical innate immune cells, such asneutrophils and macrophages in the epithelial barrier.IL-17 can respond to infectious agents rapidly byrecruiting neutrophils as the first defensive line andtriggering the production of antimicrobial peptides.3

T helper (Th)17 cells differentiate from CD4+ T cells inthe presence of transforming growth factor (TGF)-b andIL-6, and are the major source of IL-17. Recently, therehas been a growing understanding of the significanceof the Th17 subset of T cells and their relevant cytoki-nes, including IL-17, both in the biological mechanismof innate host defence and in the pathogenesis ofautoimmune or inflammatory diseases, such as psoria-sis and psoriatic arthritis.4 In addition, recent evidencehas indicated that there may be an association betweenIL-17 plasma levels and increased psoriasis risk, but theindividual published results are inconclusive.In the current study, we performed a meta-analysiswith all eligible case-control studies to obtain insightsinto these associations. This meta-analysis may pro-mote understanding of the precise role of IL-17 in theprogression of psoriasis.MethodsSearch strategyThreeinvestigators(ZX,BJ,HB)independentlysearched published studies indexed in PubMed, Web ofCorrespondence: Dr Yong Jin, School of Pharmacy, Anhui MedicalUniversity, 81 Meishan Road, Hefei, Anhui 230000, ChinaE-mail: Conflict of interest: the authors declare that they have no conflicts ofinterest.Accepted for publication 2 August 2016ScienceandMEDLINEfromtheirinceptionto1 November 2015, using the search terms 'psoriasis','IL-17' and 'interleukin-17'. Details of our searchstrategy are described in Supplementary data online.A manual search of the references of the retrieved arti-cles meeting the inclusion criteria was also performed.

Inclusion criteriaAll of the included studies had to meet all of the follow-ing four criteria: (i) study design: clinical cohort, case-control study or cross-sectional study; (ii) study subject:relationship between IL-17 plasma levels and psoriasis;(iii) patients: had to meet the diagnostic criteria forpsoriasis; and (iv) inclusion of IL-17 plasma levels.The following data were extracted: first author'sname, year of publication, total number of cases andcontrols, age and ethnicity of participants, IL-17 plasmalevels of participants, psoriasis type, measurementmethod and blood sample type. The three investigatorsextracted all data independently, complied with theselection criteria, and reached a consensus on all items.Statistical analysisCrude OR or standardized mean difference (SMD) with95% CI were calculated. Statistical significance of thepooled estimate was examined using the Z test. Power

calculations were performed using PS (Power andSample Size Calculations) software.1 Between-studyvariations and heterogeneities were estimated usingCochran Q statistic, with P < 0.05 was set as the cut-off to identify heterogeneity in the associations.1 Wealso quantified the effects of heterogeneity by usingthe I2 test (ranging from 0-100%), which representsthe proportion of interstudy variability that can becontributed to heterogeneity rather than to chance.When a significant Q test with P < 0.05 or I2 > 50%indicated the existence of heterogeneity between stud-ies, a random-effects model was conducted for themeta-analysis; otherwise, a fixed-effects model wasused. To explore potential sources of heterogeneity,subgroup analyses were performed based on ethnicityand disease type. Sensitivity analysis was performed byomitting each study in turn to assess the quality andconsistency of the results. Begger funnel plots andEgger linear regression test were used to evaluate pub-lication bias.5 Two-sided P < 0.05 was considered sta-tistically significant. All calculations were performedusing STATA software (v12.0; STATA Corp., CollegeStation, TX, USA).ResultsIn total, 164 studies matching the search terms wereidentified. Based on the inclusion criteria, 9 case-Potenally relevant arcles idenfied from search of PUBMED(n = 39) and WEB OF SCIENCE(n = 73) and MEDLINE(n = 52) and screened for retrieval Abstract review of potenally relevant arcles(n = 119) 88 potenally relevant arcles included for full length arcle review 9 arcles were included in the Meta-analysis not provided sufficient date,not case-control, or cohort study or cross-seconal study Exclusion of 45 duplicaons abstracts were clearly not cohort/case-control studies/cross-seconal study or were not conducted in

paents with psoriasis control studies1,6-13 were initially included in thismeta-analysis, and the remaining 155 were excluded.The flowchart of study selection is shown in Fig. 1,and the characteristics of the included studies areshown in Table 1.The 9 studies comprised 681 participants [345patients with psoriasis, 234 healthy controls (HCs)]who met the inclusion criteria. The publication year ofthe included studies ranged from 2005 to 2015. Threestudies were conducted in European populations andsix studies in Asian populations.Association between IL-17 plasma levels and psoriasisriskAll nine studies examined the association between themean levels of IL-17 plasma and psoriasis. Becauseheterogeneityexisted(I2 = 84.4),arandom-effectsmodel was used. The meta-analysis results revealed thatthe study from Xuan et al. on the mean levels of IL-17plasma in patients with psoriasis and HCs was an outlier(Fig. S1). Upon further analysis, we found that the mea-surement method used in that study was flow cytometrywhile the other studies used ELISA, and this differencein methods might explain the difference in results. Wetherefore excluded this article, leaving us with eightstudies on the association between the mean levels ofIL-17 plasma and psoriasis for the final analysis.Because there was obvious heterogeneity betweenthe studies (I2 = 78.7, P < 0.001), a random-effectsmodel was used. The meta-analysis results revealedthat the mean plasma levels of IL-17 in patients withpsoriasis were higher than those in HCs (SMD = 0.47,95% CI 0.07-0.86, P < 0.001) (Fig. 2). The results ofsubgroup analysis by ethnicity revealed that there wasno significant association with IL-17 plasma levels ineither the European (SMD = 0.52, 95% CI 0.08 to1.11, P = 0.03) or the Asian (SMD = 0.44, 95% CI0.14 to 1.01, P < 0.001) populations studied.

Sensitivity analysis and publication biasSensitivity analysis was performed to assess the influ-ence of each individual study on the pooled estimatesby omitting individual studies in turn. The results sug-gested that no individual study significantly affectedthe pooled estimates for the associations of plasmaIL-17 levels with psoriasis risk, and that the resultswere stable and reliable (Fig. 3).An Egger linear regression test was performed toassess the publication bias of included studies. Theshapes of the funnel plots revealed that the data fromReferenceEthinicityNumber ofpatientsAge, yearsPlasma level of IL-17PsoriasistypeSampletypeMeasurementmethodPASICasesControlsCasesControlsCasesControlsELISA, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay; FC, flow cytometry; IL, interleukin; PASI, Psoriasis Area and Severity Index; PP, pustular psoriasis; PV, psoriasis vulgaris.Association between IL-17 plasma levels and risk of psoriasis  X. Zhou et al.Kanda et al. showed some evidence of asymmetry forthe associations of plasma IL-17 levels with psoriasisrisk (Fig. S3). This may have been due to their smallsample size. The Egger test displayed strong statisticalevidence (t = 2.94, P = 0.0) for publication bias in allof the studies. Zhou et al.Discussion

Psoriasis is a common cutaneous disease whose maincharacteristic is the excessive turnover of skin cells,while activated Th cells are considered to be among itsmajor pathogenic modulators.14 Most of the evidencefor IL-17 produced by innate immune cells in the per-petuation of autoimmune and chronic inflammatorydisorders originates from rheumatoid arthritis15 andpsoriatic disease (which includes both psoriatic arthri-tis and skin psoriasis16,17), two complex conditions inwhich IL-17 targeting is at an advanced stage ofdevelopment.However,themechanismsofhowplasma levels of IL-17 raise the risk of psoriasis arenot yet clear. Therefore, we conducted a meta-analysisof all suitable studies to obtain a more accurate esti-mate of these particular associations. The aim of theanalysis was to study the correlation between plasmaIL-17 levels and psoriasis.This study is, to our knowledge, the first meta-analysis to centre upon the associations betweenplasma IL-17 levels and psoriasis. The meta-analysisincluded 8 qualifying studies, involving 345 patientswith psoriasis and 234 HCs. We found a significantdifference in mean plasma level of IL-17 betweenpatients with psoriasis and HCs. Our conclusions areconsistentwithsomepreviousstudies,indicatingthat plasma levels of IL-17 may be associated withpsoriasis.This meta-analysis has several limitations. First,only eight articles fulfilled the criteria to be includedin this meta-analysis. Second, we found during analy-sis that many of the articles used line charts and scat-ter plots to present the plasma levels of IL-17, withoutproviding mean values and standard deviations, sothat we could not extract more data from these arti-cles. Therefore, the sample size was comparativelysmall and may not have had adequate statistical datato estimate the association between IL-17 and psoria-sis risk. Thus, more studies with bigger sample sizesareneededforbetterstatisticalanalysis.Third,because a meta-analysis is a cross-sectional study, itmay include selection bias, which may have influ-enced the reliability of our findings.18 Fourth, the sta-tistical heterogeneity was high for the whole analysis.Of the nine articles we chose initially, we found thatseveral did not classify by type or severity of psoriasis,which had a negative impact on our subgroup analy-sis to find the sources of the heterogeneity. Conse-quently, detailed studies are still needed to support ourconclusions. However, based on the sensitivity analy-ses, our results were robust.

ConclusionThisstudydemonstratesthatIL-17levelisup-regulated in patients with psoriasis, which suggeststhat IL-17 may be involved in the development of pso-riasis. Because of the aforementioned limitations toour study, further studies, both epidemiological andexperimental, are warranted to clarify the associationbetween IL-17 and the development of psoriasis, andto confirm our results.What's already known about this topic?* IL-17 may be involved in the aetiology of psori-asis.* The association between IL-17 and psoriasisrisk is still ambiguous.What does this study add?* Mean plasma levels of IL-17 were higher inpatients with psoriasis than in HCs.* Overall, increased risk of psoriasis appears to beassociated with IL-17.References1 Caproni M, Antiga E, Melani L et al.
